title: The endpoint of evaporation: remnants vs information release
statement: As a black hole evaporates via Hawking radiation it shrinks toward the Planck scale, where the semiclassical description fails. What is the final state? Candidates include: a stable or long-lived Planck-mass remnant retaining the lost information; complete evaporation with information carried out in subtle correlations of the radiation; information stored in a disconnected 'baby universe'; or a genuine breakdown of unitary evolution. Each option has serious difficulties (e.g. remnants must carry unboundedly large entropy for a given mass, threatening infinite pair-production), and none is established.

originators.name: Stephen Hawking
originators.note: 1974-76 evaporation and the information question that frames the endpoint.

originators.name: Steven Giddings
originators.note: 1992 analysis of massive remnants and their problems.

ranking.rationale: Requires Planck-scale physics (low tractability) and is tightly coupled to the information paradox; ranked just below the entanglement-geometry cluster because it is more of an endgame consequence than an independent deep axiom.

key_ideas: Semiclassical gravity breaks down once the hole reaches the Planck mass (~10^-8 kg), so the true endpoint needs quantum gravity.
Stable remnant: a Planck-scale object stores the information — but must have enormous degeneracy at fixed mass, risking divergent pair-production (the 'remnant problem').
Complete evaporation + information in correlations: requires the late radiation to be highly non-thermal in a way the leading calculation misses (now supported by the island/Page-curve results, #02).
Baby universe: information escapes into a causally-disconnected region (does not help an outside observer recover it).
Long-lived (metastable) remnant as a phenomenological middle ground.
The answer is constrained by, and feeds back into, the information paradox and its island resolution.

approaches.name: Remnant scenarios
approaches.summary: Stable or long-lived Planck-scale objects retaining information; analyze consistency (pair-production, entropy bounds).

approaches.name: Complete evaporation with unitary radiation
approaches.summary: Information is in the full radiation state; supported by Page-curve / island computations.

approaches.name: Baby universes / disconnected interiors
approaches.summary: Information passes into a disconnected region; assessed against the gravitational path integral.

papers.title: Magnetic monopoles, black holes and remnants (information retention)
papers.note: Phys. Lett. B 191, 51 — early remnant proposal.

papers.title: Average entropy of a subsystem (Page)
papers.note: the Page-curve framework constraining the endpoint.

physicists.name: Don Page
physicists.contribution: Page curve constraining the final state.

simulator_angle: The simulator's thermo module gives the evaporation timescale and the shrinking horizon radius down to where semiclassical physics fails; it can illustrate the runaway final flash but not the Planck-scale endpoint.

metadata.coverage_note: Seed: 5 key references (3 with arXiv ids; Hawking/ACN as notes) + 6 physicists. Aspiration up to 25 papers, to be expanded.
